content.text: From Review Finding to Closed Remediation

Architecture reviews generate findings. A finding without a tracked remediation path is architectural entropy: the gap is known but not closing. Enterprise GenAI platforms need a structured system that moves every finding through a defined lifecycle — from initial discovery through triage, sprint commitment, deployed fix, and verified closure — with SLA gates and escalation paths at each transition.

This document defines the finding data model, the lifecycle state machine, priority-based SLAs, the exception process for findings that cannot be addressed in the standard window, and the automation that prevents findings from aging silently.

attrs.alt: Architecture finding lifecycle — states, transitions, SLA table, and escalation paths

content.text: Finding Data Model

Each finding is a structured record that captures enough context to reproduce the issue, assess its severity, assign it unambiguously, and verify closure. Findings are stored as YAML files in the tech-governance repository so they are version-controlled alongside ADRs and scorecard history.

# docs/architecture/findings/2025-07-FIND-018.yaml
id: FIND-018
title: "No output DLP filter on chat completion responses"
discovered_at: 2025-07-01
discovered_by: architecture-health-review
source: scorecard           # scorecard | adr-review | pr-review | penetration-test | incident

dimension: Security
priority: P1                # P1 | P2 | P3 | P4
status: TRIAGED             # OPEN | TRIAGED | IN_PROGRESS | IN_REVIEW | CLOSED | WONT_FIX | ESCALATED

description: >
  The /api/chat endpoint returns raw LLM output to the client without passing it
  through a DLP (Data Loss Prevention) filter. In testing, a prompt-injection
  sequence caused the model to echo a synthetic PII string that was present in
  the retrieved context. Production traffic carries real user PII in retrieved
  documents.

evidence:
  - "scorecard check 'Output DLP filter' scored FAIL in July 2025 review"
  - "proof-of-concept: src/api/chat.py:L47 — response returned without filter"

risk: >
  HIGH. PII from one user's documents could be surfaced in another user's chat
  if injected into the context. Satisfies Article 32 GDPR breach threshold.

remediation:
  owner: backend-team
  target_sprint: 2025-Q3-sprint-2
  fix_sla: 2025-07-15
  approach: >
    Implement DLP filter at the response assembly layer (src/api/middleware/dlp.py).
    Filter must: (1) apply PII regex patterns (email, SSN, credit card),
    (2) detect and redact injected secret patterns, (3) log every redaction event
    to the audit log with request_id.
  pr: null               # filled when PR is opened
  deployed_at: null      # filled on production deploy

closure:
  verified_by: null
  verification_method: "re-run scorecard check + manual regression test"
  closed_at: null

exception:
  requested: false
  approved_by: null
  expiry: null
  rationale: null
The Finding Lifecycle State Machine
Seven states cover the full lifecycle including exception and escalation paths. Transitions are gated by time SLAs or explicit approvals.
State
Entry condition
Exit to
OPEN
Finding created by any review source
TRIAGED (within triage SLA), ESCALATED (if triage SLA breached)
TRIAGED
Priority set, owner assigned, fix SLA established
IN_PROGRESS (sprint commitment), WONT_FIX (exception approved)
IN_PROGRESS
Finding committed to an active sprint
IN_REVIEW (fix deployed to staging/prod)
IN_REVIEW
Fix deployed; verification test running
CLOSED (verification passes), IN_PROGRESS (verification fails)
CLOSED
Verification test passes; scorecard check now GREEN or PASS
(terminal)
WONT_FIX
Exception request approved by required authority
(terminal, but re-evaluated on annual audit)
ESCALATED
Triage or fix SLA breached without approved exception
IN_PROGRESS (sprint committed after escalation)

IN_REVIEW is not the same as CLOSED. A finding moves to IN_REVIEW when the fix is deployed, but it only moves to CLOSED when an independent verification confirms the control is now present and effective. Skipping verification is the most common cause of finding recurrence.
Finding Tracker Implementation

The finding tracker is a lightweight Python module that reads YAML finding files, computes SLA breach dates, identifies escalations, and generates a status report. It runs daily in CI and posts a Slack digest when any finding is approaching its SLA.
The Exception (Won't Fix) Process

Some findings cannot be remediated in the standard SLA window. Accepted reasons include: the remediation requires a major architectural change that spans multiple quarters; the finding is in a system scheduled for decommission; or the risk is accepted because compensating controls reduce residual risk below the enterprise threshold. An exception does not close the finding — it records a time-limited acceptance with a mandatory re-evaluation date.

# Example exception record added to the finding YAML
exception:
  requested: true
  requested_by: backend-team-lead
  requested_at: 2025-07-08
  rationale: >
    The output DLP filter requires refactoring the response assembly pipeline,
    which is a 3-sprint effort. The system is being migrated to a new architecture
    in Q4 where DLP will be a first-class component. Interim compensating control:
    system prompt instructs the model not to repeat document content verbatim;
    input sanitisation reduces injection surface.
  compensating_controls:
    - "System prompt guardrail: no verbatim document repetition"
    - "Input scanner blocks known injection patterns"
    - "Tenant isolation prevents cross-user context assembly"
  residual_risk: MEDIUM
  approved_by: ciso-delegate
  approved_at: 2025-07-10
  expiry: 2025-10-01          # re-evaluated at quarterly review
  re_evaluation_trigger: "Q4 architecture migration complete"
P1 exceptions require CISO approval. P2 exceptions require the Architecture Review Board. P3/P4 exceptions require the platform lead.
Every exception has an expiry date. A finding that has been in WONT_FIX status past its expiry automatically reverts to OPEN at the next daily tracker run.
Compensating controls listed in the exception are added to the monthly health review agenda for the duration of the exception window.
Security P1 exceptions must be disclosed in the system's SOC 2 control environment documentation.
Verification Protocol for Closed Findings
A finding is only CLOSED when independent verification confirms the remediation is effective. The verification method depends on the finding type:
Finding type
Verification method
Evidence required
Scorecard check (FAIL → PASS)
Re-run the scorecard automated check for the specific dimension
Check script exits 0; scorecard shows PASS
Security control absent
Penetration test or security-focused code review by non-author
Signed test report; PR review from security champion
NFR threshold breach
Load test or synthetic traffic replay at target percentile
Test output showing p99 within SLA; Datadog screenshot
Tech debt / code quality
Static analysis clean run; code review sign-off
CI green; peer review approval from senior engineer
Dependency vulnerability (CVE)
pip-audit / trivy scan shows CVE resolved
Scan output attached to closing comment

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date
from typing import Callable

@dataclass
class VerificationResult:
    passed: bool
    evidence: str
    verified_by: str
    verified_at: date

def close_finding(
    finding_id: str,
    verification_fn: Callable[[], VerificationResult],
    findings_dir: str,
) -> bool:
    result = verification_fn()
    if not result.passed:
        print(f"VERIFY FAILED for {finding_id}: {result.evidence}")
        return False

    # Read, update, and re-write the YAML finding
    path = next(
        p for p in __import__("pathlib").Path(findings_dir).glob("*.yaml")
        if __import__("yaml").safe_load(p.read_text())["id"] == finding_id
    )
    raw = __import__("yaml").safe_load(path.read_text())
    raw["status"] = "CLOSED"
    raw["closure"] = {
        "verified_by": result.verified_by,
        "verification_evidence": result.evidence,
        "closed_at": result.verified_at.isoformat(),
    }
    path.write_text(__import__("yaml").dump(raw, default_flow_style=False))
    print(f"CLOSED {finding_id} — verified by {result.verified_by} on {result.verified_at}")
    return True
GitHub Issues Integration

YAML finding files are the source of truth. GitHub Issues provide the collaboration surface: PR assignment, comment threads, and sprint board placement. A sync script creates or updates a GitHub Issue for each finding and keeps the status and priority labels in sync with the YAML file.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# scripts/sync_findings_to_github.sh
# Reads all YAML findings and syncs them to GitHub Issues

set -euo pipefail

REPO="example/tech-governance"
FINDINGS_DIR="docs/architecture/findings"

for yaml_file in "$FINDINGS_DIR"/*.yaml; do
    id=$(yq '.id' "$yaml_file")
    title=$(yq '.title' "$yaml_file")
    priority=$(yq '.priority' "$yaml_file")
    status=$(yq '.status' "$yaml_file")
    dimension=$(yq '.dimension' "$yaml_file")

    # Check if issue already exists
    existing_issue=$(gh issue list --repo "$REPO" --label "finding:$id" --json number -q '.[0].number')

    if [[ -z "$existing_issue" ]]; then
        # Create new issue
        gh issue create --repo "$REPO" \
            --title "[$id] $title" \
            --label "arch-finding,priority:$priority,dim:$dimension,finding:$id" \
            --body "$(cat "$yaml_file")" \
            --assignee "$(yq '.remediation.owner' "$yaml_file")"
        echo "Created issue for $id"
    else:
        # Update labels to reflect current status
        gh issue edit "$existing_issue" --repo "$REPO" \
            --add-label "status:$status" \
            --remove-label "status:OPEN,status:TRIAGED,status:IN_PROGRESS,status:IN_REVIEW" 2>/dev/null || true

        if [[ "$status" == "CLOSED" || "$status" == "WONT_FIX" ]]; then
            gh issue close "$existing_issue" --repo "$REPO"
            echo "Closed issue #$existing_issue for $id"
        fi
    fi
done
Remediation Velocity Reporting

Monthly health reviews should include a remediation velocity slide: how many findings were opened vs. closed in the period, mean time to remediation (MTTR) by priority, and the net finding backlog trend. A growing backlog is a leading indicator of governance fatigue.
Summary
Each architecture finding is a versioned YAML record with seven fields: source, dimension, priority, status, description, evidence, and a remediation block with owner, sprint, and fix SLA.
The lifecycle has seven states — OPEN, TRIAGED, IN_PROGRESS, IN_REVIEW, CLOSED, WONT_FIX, ESCALATED — with SLA-gated transitions and explicit escalation owners at each priority level.
P1 Security findings must be triaged within 24h and fixed within 14 days; P4 tech debt has a 180-day fix SLA. SLA breach without an approved exception triggers escalation.

Exceptions require compensating controls, an expiry date, and approval from the authority appropriate to the priority (CISO for P1, ARB for P2). Expired exceptions automatically revert findings to OPEN.
Closure requires independent verification matching the finding type — scorecard re-run, penetration test, load test, or CVE scan — not just a deployed PR.
A daily CI job computes SLA breach status, syncs findings to GitHub Issues, and posts a Slack digest for findings due within 7 days.
Remediation velocity (MTTR by priority and backlog trend) is tracked monthly and is a primary input to the architecture health review.
